title: From Independent to Joint: Enhancing Quantum Phase and Correlation Factor Estimation by Squeezed Reservoir Engineering
authors: Cai-Hong Liao, Yan-Ling Li, Long Huang, Xing Xiao
abstract: High-precision quantum parameter estimation is fundamental to the advancement of quantum metrology. Although reservoir engineering provides a powerful approach to improve estimation by tailoring system-environment interactions, the role of the squeezing phase and correlations arising from the sequential utilization of the same squeezed reservoir remains inadequately explored. In this work, we employ a correlated squeezed-thermal reservoir to enhance the precision of estimating the phase parameter $φ$ and the correlation factor $μ$, both individually and simultaneously. We show that the squeezing phase $Φ$ is crucial for achieving quantum-enhanced precision, with optimal phase-matching conditions that depend strongly on $μ$. Specifically, we derive the near-optimal phase-matching relations aimed at maximizing the quantum Fisher information (QFI) for both $φ$ and $μ$, as well as minimizing the total variance $Δ_{\rm sim}$ in joint estimation. Furthermore, we show that the joint estimation variance is dominated by $F_φ$, which motivates our search for the phase-matching conditions that minimize $Δ_{\text{sim}}$. Through the ratio $R$ of variances, we demonstrate that joint estimation conserves quantum resources and maintains high precision when the squeezing phase is optimized for $F_φ$, despite the inherent incompatibility of the parameters. These findings provide practical insights into reservoir engineering strategies for high-precision quantum sensing and information processing.
summary: This paper investigates how to improve the precision of estimating quantum phase and correlation parameters using a squeezed thermal reservoir. By manipulating the squeezing phase and strength, the authors find specific 'phase-matching' conditions that maximize information retrieval. They specifically show that even when estimating multiple parameters simultaneously, one can achieve high precision by prioritizing the optimization of the phase parameter. The results suggest that reservoir engineering is a powerful tool for high-precision quantum sensing.
main_problem: Enhancing the precision of joint estimation for a quantum phase parameter and a correlation factor in a system interacting with a correlated squeezed-thermal reservoir.
main_result: The study identifies optimal squeezing phase-matching conditions to maximize precision and demonstrates that joint estimation can maintain high precision and conserve resources even when parameters are incompatible.
method: The authors utilize the Quantum Fisher Information Matrix (QFIM) and the quantum Cramér-Rao bound to analyze estimation precision and compare independent versus joint estimation strategies.
model_or_system: Two identical two-level systems (qubits) passing successively through an optical cavity, interacting with a common correlated squeezed-thermal reservoir.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) for phase (F_phi) and correlation (F_mu), total variance (Delta_sim), and the precision ratio (R).
important_parameters: Phase parameter (phi), correlation factor (mu), squeezing strength (r), squeezing phase (Phi), and reservoir temperature (T).
main_assumptions: Identical qubits, rotating-wave approximation, and the use of a squeezed thermal reservoir model.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the physical model and reservoir limits; Figure 3 and 4 show the behavior and improvement of QFI relative to the squeezing phase; Figure 10 shows total variance versus time at different temperatures; Figure 11 identifies phase-matching conditions; Figure 12 shows the advantage of joint estimation via the ratio R.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of parameter estimation in correlated reservoirs, defines the physical model and master equations, derives phase-matching conditions for individual and joint estimation, and concludes with a numerical analysis of the advantages of joint estimation.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum metrology as it provides specific strategies (reservoir engineering via squeezing phase) to mitigate decoherence and optimize multi-parameter sensing in the presence of correlated noise.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

(b) Mapping diagrams for a two-qubit system under uncorrelated and fully correlated conditions.
